_meta.description: Discovered questions from SERP, PAA, forums, and GBP Q&A — with answered/gap status

questions.question: How long do I have to file a car accident lawsuit in Georgia?
questions.notes: SOL answer exists on drunk driver sub-type page FAQ but NOT on car accident pillar or Savannah intersection page. High PAA frequency. O.C.G.A. § 9-3-33 (2 years). Critical for intersection page FAQ.

questions.question: Do I need a lawyer for a car accident in Savannah, GA?
questions.notes: High-intent investigational query. No dedicated answer on Savannah intersection page. Multiple competitor pages target this exact question. Strong conversion potential — answers should emphasize contingency fee, free consultation.

questions.question: How much does a car accident attorney cost in Savannah, Georgia?
questions.notes: Cost/fee intent query. No prominent answer on Savannah intersection page. Answer: contingency fee ~33%, no fee unless we win. High conversion value. Georgia Bar rules apply.

questions.question: What happens if the at-fault driver has no insurance in Georgia?
questions.notes: Competitor pages (Spiva Law, John Foy, Evans Injury Attorneys) specifically target Savannah uninsured motorist. UM coverage in GA required to be offered, ~12.4% of GA drivers uninsured. Exists on rideshare sub-type but not pillar/intersection FAQ.

questions.question: Should I give a recorded statement to the insurance company after a car accident in Georgia?
questions.notes: High-frequency question found in competitor FAQs. Answer: No — not legally required to give recorded statement to other driver's insurer; adjusters use statements to reduce claims. No current Roden Law FAQ addresses this.

questions.question: How long does a car accident settlement take in Georgia?
questions.notes: Common post-accident timeline question. GA insurers have 40 days to respond to claims. Simple cases: months; complex: 1-2 years. No current Roden Law page answers this directly. Strong AI-citation potential for featured snippet.

questions.question: What is Georgia's modified comparative fault rule and how does it affect my car accident claim?
questions.notes: Exists in body content of rear-end collision sub-type (O.C.G.A. § 51-12-33) but not as a dedicated FAQ on the pillar or Savannah intersection. Lower priority than transactional queries but important for E-E-A-T.

questions.question: Who is liable in a truck accident in South Carolina — the driver, the trucking company, or both?
questions.notes: Highest-value question this cycle. Multiple parties may be liable: driver, trucking company (vicarious liability), cargo company, truck manufacturer. Competitor pages (Derrick Law, Bringardner) prominently feature this. No dedicated FAQ on Charleston truck accident intersection page.

questions.question: How is a truck accident case different from a car accident case in South Carolina?
questions.notes: Covered on 18-wheeler sub-type page but not on Charleston truck accident intersection page. Key distinctions: FMCSA regs, higher insurance limits ($750K-$5M), black box data, multiple liable parties, longer investigation. High AI-citation potential.

questions.question: How long does a truck accident settlement take in South Carolina?
questions.notes: PAA question on competitor sites. Simple cases: months; complex multi-party cases: 1-3 years. SC SOL is 3 years (S.C. Code § 15-3-530). No existing Roden Law answer on Charleston truck accident intersection page.

questions.question: What federal trucking regulations apply to my South Carolina truck accident case?
questions.notes: Now covered in rewritten blog post 1661 (blog-commercial-truck-accidents-who-is-at-fault) with dedicated FMCSA regulations section. Also on fatigue and ELD sub-type pages.

questions.question: How much insurance are trucking companies required to carry under federal law?
questions.notes: Now covered in rewritten blog post 1661 (blog-commercial-truck-accidents-who-is-at-fault) with dedicated insurance requirements table and FAQ. Also on 18-wheeler sub-type.

questions.question: Who can file a wrongful death claim in South Carolina?
questions.notes: SC-specific rule: only estate personal representative (executor/administrator) may file, not family members directly (S.C. Code § 15-51-20). Covered generally on sub-type pages but the SC-specific nuance (no direct family filing) is not featured on the Columbia intersection page. High AI-citation potential.

questions.question: What is the statute of limitations for wrongful death in South Carolina?
questions.notes: 3 years from date of death (S.C. Code § 15-3-530), but 2 years for government defendants. Both deadlines critical to cite. Covered on WD sub-type pages but NOT on Columbia wrongful death intersection page as a dedicated FAQ. High-urgency transactional query.

questions.question: What damages can a family recover in a South Carolina wrongful death lawsuit?
questions.notes: Competitor pages prominently feature this. Recoverable: lost income/benefits, medical/funeral expenses, loss of companionship, pain and suffering, punitive damages. SC caps malpractice non-economic damages at $350K per provider. Covered on sub-types but not Columbia intersection FAQ.

questions.question: Can family members file a wrongful death lawsuit directly in South Carolina, or does it have to go through the estate?
questions.notes: Critical jurisdictional nuance that surprises most families. SC requires estate personal representative to file (S.C. Code § 15-51-20). Spouse, children, and parents are beneficiaries but cannot file directly. Multiple competitor pages (Berger Law, McKinney Tucker) specifically answer this. Not addressed on any Roden Law intersection or pillar page.

questions.question: How much is a wrongful death case worth in South Carolina?
questions.notes: Very high-intent query. Multiple competitor pages rank for this. Answer should address: no set average; depends on liability strength, income, dependents, pain and suffering; SC punitive damages capped at $500K or 3x actual. No Roden Law page answers this for Columbia/SC context.

content_gaps.description: Car accident intersection pages (Savannah, and likely all cities) lack dedicated FAQ sections covering: SOL, attorney cost, do-I-need-a-lawyer, settlement timeline, uninsured drivers, and recorded statements.
Charleston truck accident intersection page lacks FAQ covering liability parties, case timeline, and FMCSA regulations — key differentiators vs. car accident cases.
Columbia wrongful death intersection page lacks SC-specific FAQ about who can file (estate personal representative only), statute of limitations, and case valuation.
